segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment presents a dynamic visual journey through Francisco Goya’s artistic evolution, beginning with formal royal portraits and transitioning into darker, more introspective and chaotic works. It opens with a close-up of 'Fernando VII' (1814), a regal portrait showing the king in a white uniform with a gold-embellished sash and a high white cravat, set against a dark background. This is followed by 'Joseph Bonaparte' (1808), a full-length portrait of the French king in opulent white and gold regalia, seated on a throne with a golden drape behind him, exuding imperial authority. The sequence then shifts to Goya’s self-portraits: first, a youthful 'Self-Portrait' (1771) with dark hair and a somber expression, then a later, more somber 'Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta' (1820), where Goya appears frail and ill, supported by a doctor holding a glass, reflecting his physical decline. The tone darkens further with 'Two Old Men' (1819–1823), a haunting depiction of two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, rendered in thick, expressive brushstrokes. The narrative intensifies with 'The Second of May 1808' (1814), a chaotic battle scene filled with horses, soldiers, and civilians in violent struggle, capturing the horror of war. This is intercut with 'The Marquise de la Solana' (1793), a delicate portrait of a woman in a white shawl with a pink flower in her hair, providing a brief contrast. The segment then transitions to Goya’s etchings: 'And They are Like Wild Beasts' (published 1863), a stark black-and-white print depicting violent, animalistic figures, and 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters' (1799), showing a sleeping man surrounded by owls and bats, symbolizing the dangers of ignorance. The final image, 'A Way of Flying' (published 1864), shows a grotesque figure with wings and a long tongue, flying over a landscape, reinforcing Goya’s descent into the surreal and nightmarish. The sequence traces Goya’s artistic arc from courtly elegance to profound psychological and social critique, marked by increasing darkness and emotional intensity.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a formal portrait of Napoleon by Paul Delaroche, depicting him in a dark military uniform, gold epaulettes, and medals against a deep red background, as the narrator states that "Napoleon came in with talks of restoring democracy." The scene then shifts to Benoît-Luis Prévost's allegorical engraving 'Truth, Light, Philosophy and Reason,' depicting robed figures symbolizing enlightenment, aligning with the audio explaining that Goya and his liberal friends initially supported the French Revolution, seeing French rule as a "great hope of liberalization and enlightenment." This is followed by Eugenio Lucas Velázquez's dark painting 'Condemned by The Inquisition,' a nightmarish scene of torture, as the narrator highlights that "one of the first things Napoleon did was to abolish the hated Inquisition." Goya’s stark etching 'And There’s Nothing to be Done' then appears, showing a man over a fallen figure, reflecting the audio's description of Goya's changing opinion of the French after witnessing the "violent subjugation of his countrymen." This is followed by Goya’s dark and violent oil painting 'Attack of a Woman,' depicting an assault by a monstrous figure, underscoring how "the war and its ensuing atrocities affected him and his work deeply." Next, Goya's grotesque etching 'Simpleton’s Folly' shows a man clutching his head in madness, illustrating the "darker subject matter" and "deep fear for Spain's future" mentioned in the audio. The video then shows Roque Gameiro’s colorful painting 'Guerrilla Warfare,' a dynamic scene of Spanish rebels ambushing French troops, directly illustrating the audio's mention of "Spanish rebels [starting] a campaign of guerrilla warfare." This is followed by Goya’s powerful 'The Third of May 1808,' depicting a man before a firing squad, as the audio notes that "eventually Napoleon would be forced out in 1813." The segment concludes with José Aparicio’s grand, celebratory painting 'Landing of Ferdinand VII in El Puerto,' showing the king's return, symbolizing restoration, as the audio confirms "King Ferdinand the Seventh, who had been living in exile."

This is followed by a dark, dramatic painting, 'Condemned by The Inquisition' by Eugenio Lucas Velázquez (1860), depicting a nightmarish scene of torture and persecution, with figures in conical hats and chains, illuminated by flickering torchlight. The sequence continues with Francisco Goya’s etching 'And There’s Nothing to be Done (Plate 15)' (published 1864), a stark black-and-white image of a man standing over a fallen figure, evoking despair and helplessness. Then, Goya’s 'Attack of a Woman' (1811) appears, a dark, violent oil painting showing a woman being assaulted by a monstrous figure, with another figure in the background holding a torch. This is followed by 'Simpleton’s Folly' (1799), a grotesque etching of a man in a state of madness, clutching his head. The video then shows 'Guerrilla Warfare' by Roque Gameiro (1917), a colorful, dynamic scene of Spanish guerrillas ambushing French troops in a rocky landscape. The next image is Goya’s 'The Third of May 1808' (1814), a powerful depiction of a man with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies, under a dark sky. The segment concludes with 'Landing of Ferdinand VII in El Puerto' by José Aparicio (1823), a grand, celebratory painting of the king’s return, with nobles, soldiers, and civilians gathered on a ship, under a bright sky, symbolizing restoration and hope.

The segment concludes with a stark black-and-white etching, '2. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Francisco Goya’s stark black-and-white etching, 'No se puede mirar' (It Cannot Be Looked At), depicting a grotesque, agonized figure, as a male narrator explicitly states the title and translates it, emphasizing that 'one cannot look at this.' The narration continues, explaining how some of Goya's work remains 'hard to look at' and how his deafness fostered a 'more experimental vein,' allowing him freedom beyond patron demands, even if his 'darker work' was censored. This context frames the subsequent etchings like 'This is Worse,' showing a man in despair with a knife-wielding figure, and the somber oil painting 'Two Old Men,' featuring emaciated figures. A shift occurs with the bright, pastoral 'The Parasol,' which the narrator notes was painted 'just before Goya was struck deaf,' followed by the subtle sound of a quill pen scratching. The mood then darkens again with the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath' and the violent etching 'They Do Not Want To,' as the narrator explains that works like these were painted 'after' his deafness, and from 1792 on, Goya's 'private world would be stripped of color and light.' A cheerful 'The Game of Horse and Rider' briefly appears before the final, extended scene: 'Yard With Lunatics (Corral de Locos).' This dark, haunting oil painting of agitated figures in a confined space is highlighted by the narrator, who reveals Goya saw such scenes in the asylum of Zaragoza, all while a somber, classical string piece plays in the background, underscoring the segment's exploration of Goya's evolving, often disturbing, artistic vision.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment presents a curated sequence of Francisco Goya’s artworks, transitioning from dark, etched prints to oil paintings, showcasing the evolution of his style and thematic depth. It begins with a close-up of an etching titled 'No se puede mirar' (It Cannot Be Looked At), depicting a grotesque, contorted figure in agony, rendered in stark black-and-white with heavy cross-hatching. This is followed by 'This is Worse' (1864), another etching showing a man in despair clutching his head, with a figure behind him holding a knife, emphasizing psychological torment. The sequence shifts to 'Two Old Men' (1819–1823), a somber oil painting of two emaciated figures sharing a meager meal, their faces gaunt and expressive, painted in thick, textured strokes. Next, 'The Parasol' (1777) appears—a bright, pastoral scene with a woman in a blue and yellow dress holding a green parasol, accompanied by a man in a brown jacket, set against a serene landscape. The mood darkens again with 'Witches’ Sabbath' (1819–1823), a chaotic, nightmarish oil painting of grotesque figures surrounding a demonic goat-like creature, rendered in dark, swirling brushwork. The segment continues with 'They Do Not Want To' (1864), an etching of a man being attacked while another figure raises a knife, echoing themes of violence and helplessness. A brief, blurred transition leads to 'The Game of Horse and Rider' (1792), a cheerful painting of children playing, one riding on another’s shoulders, set against a light, airy background. The final and most extended scene is 'Yard With Lunatics (Corral de Locos)' (1794), a dark, haunting oil painting depicting a group of agitated, half-naked figures in a dimly lit, confined space, some embracing, others screaming or gesturing wildly, with a single light source from a barred window illuminating the chaos. The painting’s oppressive atmosphere and raw emotional intensity dominate the segment’s conclusion.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands meticulously engraving fine lines into a metal plate using a sharp etching tool, a process the narrator describes as "composing the surface of the plate." This labor-intensive technique, 'Engraving directly on to metal,' is then contrasted in a split-screen with 'Engraving on soft ground,' where, as the narrator explains, drawings are etched into a soft waxy ground rather than directly onto the metal. An expressive etching of a figure raising arms exemplifies the soft ground technique, characterized by the narrator as producing a "clean, delicate, intricate, and spontaneous" line, akin to drawing or watercolor. Historical prints, including Jan Norblin's 'Ecce Homo' (1770s) and a self-portrait etching by Goya, are shown side-by-side with their copper plates, illustrating the narrator's point that "the etching is done back to front, including all the writing... as the final print will be the reversed image." The process continues with a hand submerging a metal plate into a steaming acid bath, as the narrator states, "Once the design is etched, the plate is immersed in a bath of acid." He elaborates that areas protected by wax remain untouched, but "the lines that were etched and so exposed by the artist get attacked by the acid," leaving the design "embedded in the surface in grooves which will hold ink." A hand then scrubs the plate with a brush to reveal the etched image. The final frames show a close-up comparison of an original etching and its copper plate, emphasizing intricate lines and textures, with the narrator concluding that "gradations of line tone can be achieved by varying the length of time in the acid bath," as "longer periods produce more deeply bitten areas."

He clarifies that this initial engraving is the first stage before adding aquatint tones, which create tonal effects rather than lines. As the narrator states, "Here we can see three stages of an aquatint print," the hand holds the historical print up to the light, illustrating how one tone is added, and then another. The scene shifts to a modern, well-lit studio where Roisin, identified by on-screen text, grinds a yellowish substance in a white mortar with a pestle. The narrator identifies this as "powdered rosin," an acid-resistant material used to create tonal areas, which is attached by heating. Roisin then applies this ground to a copper plate with a brush. The narrator explains that the plate is then immersed in an acid bath, just like when etching, where the acid eats into the metal around the dust particles. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a side-by-side comparison of an etching before and after aquatint, visually demonstrating the transformation from a stark line drawing to a tonal, textured image with added depth. A male narrator explains that aquatint creates a "grainy sandpaper-like surface" that holds ink to give the effect of an "area of wash." This is followed by a close-up of a detailed etching by Julius Caesar Ibbetson, depicting a pastoral scene with a donkey carrying a family, illustrating the atmospheric effects of aquatint. As melancholic classical music, possibly played on a string instrument, begins, the scene transitions to a full view of Francisco Goya’s 1799 etching 'Can’t Anyone Unleash Us? audio_updated_entities.The Rope is Breaking Etching: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) showing a man in a robe falling from a rope, surrounded by a crowd of heads, rendered with dramatic shading and expressive lines, symbolizing collapse and chaos. The narration identifies the falling figure as a priest walking on a frayed and tattered rope, and a woman's voice cries out, 'The rope is breaking!' The narrator further explains that Goya, in a rare act of self-censorship, downgraded the figure from the Pope to a priest in this print.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Francisco Goya’s dark and expressive etching *Will She Live Again?*, depicting a woman surrounded by shadowy figures, as a male narrator describes Goya's work as universal, showing deep compassion for all victims of war and an understanding of suffering that affects humanity. The scene transitions to another etching, *No hay quien los socorra* (Plate 31), where a lone figure stands over fallen bodies in a desolate landscape, with the Spanish text circled in red. The narrator emphasizes this image, stating a lone survivor is faced with devastation and cries out in desperation, "No hay quien lo socorra," which he translates as "There is no one to help them." A profile sketch of Goya’s face appears, followed by a scene from the 1971 film *Goya or the Hard Way to the Enlightenment*, showing a dimly lit studio with etchings, as the narrator explains Goya began these etchings in secret at 62, finishing them under Ferdinand VII, but they weren't published until 35 years after his death because they were deemed "too incendiary," denouncing both violence and abuse of power. The video returns to Goya’s etchings, including *Not (In This Case) Either* and *What Courage*, visually embodying the suffering and defiance Goya captured. A brief shot of a museum corridor lined with framed paintings and a close-up of a man in a dark coat precedes a formal portrait of Joseph Bonaparte, seated on a golden throne in opulent royal attire, symbolizing the imperial authority and French occupation period that Goya navigated, as the narrator concludes that Ferdinand's censorship and dislike led to a most difficult time for Goya, with his commissions drying up.

The scene transitions to a wider view of another etching, *No hay quien los socorra* (Plate 31), depicting a lone figure standing over two fallen bodies in a desolate landscape, with the Spanish text circled in red to emphasize its meaning: 'There is no one to help them.' This is followed by a profile sketch of Goya’s face, rendered in fine, expressive lines, before shifting to a scene from the 1971 film *Goya or the Hard Way to the Enlightenment*, showing a dimly lit studio with etchings hanging from the ceiling and a man standing in contemplation. The video then returns to Goya’s etchings, including *Not (In This Case) Either* and *What Courage*, both from 1864, depicting figures in moments of suffering and defiance. A brief shot of a museum corridor lined with framed paintings leads to a close-up of a man in a dark coat, possibly a museum visitor, before transitioning to a formal portrait of Joseph Bonaparte, seated on a golden throne in opulent white and gold royal attire, holding a scepter and orb, symbolizing imperial authority. The sequence ends with a close-up of the portrait, emphasizing the regal and dramatic lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a sepia-toned historical photograph of the Quinta del Sordo, Goya’s later residence, showing three figures before its ivy-covered facade, as a male narrator explains that in 1819, at 73, Goya moved to this farmhouse in the countryside. This transitions to a top-down scale model of the Quinta del Sordo from 1828, while the narrator details how Goya, isolated from the Spanish court, created extraordinary images, even though he could still see the Royal Palace from his house, allowing him to create politically charged works while overlooking his former life of privilege. As the narrator states Goya was 'free to create monsters,' a dark, unsettling string melody begins, accompanying an original 1874 photograph of 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' a dark, unsettling painting from the farmhouse. The camera then dramatically zooms on Saturn's monstrous, wild-eyed face as he consumes his child. This grotesque image is jarringly contrasted with an overlay of the formal, brightly lit portrait 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis Painting.' The scene returns to a close-up of 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' emphasizing its dark, visceral brushwork, with the text '8. The Black Paintings (1819-1823)' appearing on screen. The narrator mentions 'Los Caprichos and the disasters of war' as horrific images Goya withdrew due to fear of reprisal, but with the Black Paintings, he was isolated enough from Madrid to express himself exactly how he wanted, safe from prying eyes. The segment concludes with a detail from 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' depicting dark, contorted figures in a nightmarish scene, reinforcing the oppressive atmosphere of Goya’s late, disturbing works, all set to the continuous somber music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dimly lit, sepia-toned digital reconstruction of the Quinta del Sordo, showcasing large, dark paintings like 'Two Old Men Eating Soup' and 'Witches’ Sabbath' on its walls, as a narrator explains Goya produced an extraordinary cycle of 14 works painted directly onto the wallpaper of his house's two floors. A transparent 3D wireframe model overlays the room, gradually filling with images of Goya’s Black Paintings, illustrating their spatial arrangement, while the narrator emphasizes Goya lived like a hermit, never mentioning, signing, or titling these provocative works, which only his maid saw. The scene transitions to archival black-and-white photographs from 1874, labeled 'Original Photo from the Farmhouse', showing damaged versions of 'Saturn Devouring His Son' and 'Witches’ Sabbath', reinforcing the narrator's point that Goya created them as fresco paintings, never expecting them to be displayed publicly. A clip from the 1971 film 'Goya or the Hard Way to Enlightenment' then shows a man observing a large painting in a dimly lit studio, before a title card '9. Removing the Paintings' appears, accompanied by melancholy acoustic guitar music. A portrait of Baron Émile d’Erlanger is shown as the narrator reveals he bought the house in 1863, long after Goya's death. The segment concludes with grainy black-and-white archival footage of the 'Strappo' technique, where two men meticulously peel the fragile paint layers from the walls, illustrating the delicate and labor-intensive conservation process that Baron d’Erlanger initiated to transfer the paintings from the walls onto canvas, a task the narrator likens to the impossible delicacy of stripping wallpaper.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with grainy, black-and-white archival footage of Goya's 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Réunion de sorciers', displayed in their original damaged frames with visible archival labels, as a male narrator explains that "panels were lost, and sections had to be repaired by restorers, and some were purposely altered for aesthetic reasons." The scene transitions to a detailed architectural drawing of a gallery layout, showing the spatial arrangement of Goya’s Black Paintings, including the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting' and 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', within a reconstructed room, while the narrator notes that "Luckily for posterity, the paintings were photographed in situ in 1874, before they were removed." This shifts to a modern, dimly lit museum gallery, labeled '10. Context', where visitors observe large, dark Black Paintings, including the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', under focused lighting, as the narrator emphasizes that "thanks to these images, we have a record of where they were, and what condition they were in," accompanied by a soft, melancholic acoustic guitar melody. A close-up of the 'Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta Painting', depicting a frail Goya supported by Doctor Arrieta, is shown as the narrator states, "There's more than one way to look at these paintings. I think the deafness, madness angle has been overstated," continuing to assert that Goya "had been deaf for over 30 years at this point, and there is no evidence of madness in his letters or elsewhere." The video then cuts to a sepia-toned film clip from Abel Gance’s 1927 *Napoleon*, depicting a chaotic crowd scene, as the narrator urges, "We have to see them in the context of political and religious turmoil," with the background music shifting to a dramatic, urgent orchestral score. This is followed by a series of Goya’s Black Paintings: 'Two Men Fighting', 'A Man with a Winged Monster', and 'The Women Laughing Painting', while the narrator describes them as works "by an old, tired man, stripped of his status and wealth, disappointed and angry with the world, and desperate to express it in the only way he knows how. In paint." The sequence culminates in a grid montage of multiple Black Paintings, including 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting', 'Altropos (The Fates) Painting', and 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis Painting', layered with the 'Self-Portrait at an Easel Painting' and 'Majo With Guitar (Detail) Painting', creating a dense, overlapping visual narrative, as the narrator states, "There are 14 of them in total..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a grid montage of Francisco Goya’s dark, expressive paintings, including the visceral 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting', the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting', and the somber 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', as a male narrator explains that these works are linked technically, with Goya using a thick layer of black paint directly onto plaster walls as a base, then creating characters with lighter shades of whites, grays, ochres, and earth. The oil palette is described as very limited, with Goya's brushwork being broad, energetic, and loose, echoing a morally chaotic world, and impressionistic long before the Impressionists existed. The sequence transitions to a close-up of the emaciated figures in the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', followed by a detailed zoom into the grotesque, distorted grins of the two women in the 'Women Laughing Painting'. The scene then shifts to a museum gallery where visitors gather around a large, dark painting, identified by on-screen text as '11. Pilgrimage to San Isadora', as the narrator invites viewers to look at the theories behind the 'Black Paintings' one by one, accompanied by melancholic acoustic guitar music. The camera focuses on the frenzied crowd and contorted faces of 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting', which the narrator identifies as showing the passage of Goya's disillusionment and being the exact same subject matter painted by Goya 30 years previously. This is followed by a contrasting cut to 'The Meadow of San Isidro' by Goya (1788), a vibrant, sunlit festival scene with a panoramic view of Madrid, labeled with on-screen text 'The Meadow of San Isidro, Francisco Goya, 1788'. The narrator explains that this earlier work, painted in 1788, depicts the feast of Saint Isidora and is full of light and joy, before the video concludes with a return to the dark 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting', reinforcing the stark thematic shift in Goya’s vision.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a historical reenactment or film clip depicting a bustling colonial-era scene, featuring soldiers in blue and white uniforms, horses, and a crowd around a wooden platform, all set to an ominous, low background music. A narrator explains that "30 years later, that world had become a nightmare," detailing "30 years, which saw the French Revolution, the rise and fall of Napoleon, the occupation of Spain, and the horrendous guerrilla war that followed," along with "the extension of the Inquisition, and the restoration of a dictator king." Visually, this transitions to a formal portrait of Napoleon Bonaparte, seated in a white military uniform with a green overcoat, exuding authority and contemplation. The scene then shifts to a chaotic battle painting with mounted warriors, followed by a dramatic, fiery religious or ceremonial event with figures in ornate robes and flames, all while the intense background music continues. The narrative then moves to a panoramic, light-filled painting, 'The Meadow of San Isidro,' depicting a festive gathering with elegantly dressed figures relaxing on a hillside overlooking Madrid. The narrator describes this as "a celebration, full of young people dancing and drinking and having fun," accompanied by a lighter, more festive, brass band-like tune. This is starkly contrasted with 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting,' a dark, grim depiction of a crowd in distress, showing figures in anguish and despair. The narrator explains, "The later version is the exact same scene, but the sky has turned from glorious sunshine to dark and stormy," as the music becomes dissonant, darker, and unsettling. The two paintings are shown side-by-side, emphasizing the contrast. Close-ups of the dark painting highlight the contorted, agonized faces of the crowd, while the narrator adds, "Where once the revelers sang along to brass bands, the source of the music is now a lone madman," and "The once orderly crowd are now a seething mass of madness, no longer laughing, but howling in agony," with the unsettling music intensifying into a distinct, wailing sound. Finally, a split-screen juxtaposes the formal portrait of Napoleon with the dark, suffering crowd, drawing a thematic connection between imperial power and human suffering. The narrator concludes, "And the reason for their pain is right in the middle of the mass of bodies, staring out at us," before naming "Napoleon, Bonaparte," as the wailing sound peaks and then fades slightly, reinforcing the video’s exploration of historical violence and social contrast through close-ups of the distressed faces.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Goya's 'Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro,' a dark, expressive painting depicting a frenzied crowd with contorted, screaming faces, rendered in thick, chaotic brushwork. A male speaker begins by discussing the identification of Goya's black paintings, contradicting the belief that they lacked real meaning or were merely products of Goya's psychiatric decline. As the camera slowly zooms out to reveal the full composition of the 'Pilgrimage,' showing a long procession under a stormy sky, solemn, traditional Spanish string music begins to play. The speaker identifies this painting as 'another procession to the sanctuary of San Isidro,' noting its proximity to Goya's farmhouse, and adds that it's also known as 'the Inquisition.' The scene then transitions to 'Burial of the Sardine,' a chaotic, celebratory crowd with figures in white dresses and grotesque masks under a dark sky, which the speaker refers to as 'the lighter-toned Burial of the Sardine.' This is followed by 'A Procession of the Flagellants,' showing bloodied, conical-hatted figures whipping themselves, which the speaker calls 'the dark and cruel Procession of the Flagellants.' The speaker explains that for Spanish liberals like Goya, these ceremonies represented 'ignorance and superstition,' something from the past. The video returns to the 'Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro,' focusing on a group of grim-faced women in dark shawls, one holding a small object, with a white outline highlighting a specific woman. The speaker emphasizes Goya's portrayal of these themes by dressing the figures in '17th-century fashions' and describes a prominent man in the painting, dressed in 17th-century clothes with a mustache and goatee, holding a sword and a roll of paper. The overall atmosphere remains dark, oppressive, and emotionally charged, reflecting Goya’s signature style.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a dark, dramatic religious painting, where a robed figure, identified by a speaker as an 'inquisitor' in a uniquely Spanish 'gollia' collar and necklace, holds a cross over two seated individuals: a praying woman in yellow and a man in dark clothing. The speaker notes the inquisitor is colluding with a 'friar' next to him, and the surrounding characters are 'Celestinas or procuresses,' whom Goya often portrayed as 'old women who exploit young girls.' This thematic context is visually reinforced as the camera shifts to 'Maja and Celestine on a Balcony,' depicting a young woman in a golden dress with an older man. The somber, dimly lit atmosphere, which the speaker explains Goya used to emphasize 'superstitious beliefs' by shrouding main characters in darkness against a distant light, continues through a wide shot of 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro,' showing a long procession of figures against a stormy sky, and a close-up of women with fearful or ecstatic expressions. A somber, string-based background music begins as the video cuts to the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath' painting, featuring a demonic figure surrounded by grotesque, writhing forms. A speaker highlights this work as an example of restoration alterations, explaining that the original 'Witches’ Sabbath' was 'much wider' before a restorer removed the canvas sides, ignoring Goya's use of empty space for dramatic effect and changing the 'center of balance' by positioning a 'woman in white' nearer the middle. This alteration is visually demonstrated through a split-screen and grid comparison of the painting in various states, revealing archival black-and-white versions with visible damage, fading, and frame issues alongside the current, restored color versions.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a close-up of a dark, dramatic painting depicting a religious scene: a robed figure holding a cross over two seated individuals, one of whom is a woman in a yellow robe with a white headdress, her hands clasped in prayer, while the other is a man in dark clothing with a gold chain. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Francisco Goya's 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' focusing on the central demonic figure, identified by the speaker as 'the he-goat or Satan.' This dark, horned, goat-like creature, with a single visible eye, is described as preaching from a small mound with its mouth wide open, giving instructions to the 'old crones' and 'obsequious women with bestial twisted faces, thought to be witches' that surround it. The speaker notes the figure is dressed as a priest, making an anti-clerical statement. The camera slowly pans right, revealing more of the chaotic, nightmarish scene with grotesque, writhing human figures. The scene transitions to Goya's 'Witches’ Flight' (1797), showing figures flailing in mid-air against a black background, followed by his black-and-white etching 'Gone for Good' (1799), depicting a flying woman with the Latin inscription 'Volaverunt.' The video then returns to a full view of the 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' showing the horned figure adorned with a leafy crown amidst a crowd of monstrous figures in a desolate landscape. The speaker provides context, explaining that Spain was marked by the Enlightenment, fervent Catholicism, and a widespread belief in the supernatural, with most people believing in witches, and that Goya had used witchcraft imagery before. The scene abruptly changes to José de Ribera’s dark, tenebrist painting 'Ixion' (1632), with the word 'Tenebrism' overlaid, highlighting the dramatic chiaroscuro. The speaker explains that Goya's use of tenebrism was popularized by 17th-century Spanish artists like Ribera, whom Goya admired. The final shot is a close-up of Rembrandt’s 'Lucretia' (1666), a somber portrait of a woman in a white and gold dress holding a dagger, her face illuminated against a dark background, with text identifying the title and artist, presented as an example of Rembrandt, Goya's hero, also using tenebrism to heighten dramatic effect.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a dark, expressive oil painting, identified as a Black Painting by Francisco Goya, depicting two grotesque, bearded figures in a nightmarish embrace, rendered in thick, textured brushwork with deep browns and ochres, evoking horror and decay. A narrator immediately highlights a 'demon figure's communication,' explaining that the smaller, demonic-looking figure clinging to the shoulder of the tall, gaunt figure is 'shouting,' suggesting this could be an allusion to Goya's deafness. As the camera slowly zooms out to reveal the full composition, the narrator posits that the tall figure with a long white beard and dark robes, holding a staff, is a 'self-portrait of Goya,' who was well into his 70s. The scene transitions to a split-screen comparison, with the full painting on the left and a monochrome sketch of a similar bearded figure with a staff on the right, suggesting a preparatory study. The narrator mentions that Goya didn't name his Black Paintings, leading to various titles over the years, including 'the two monks' for this work, due to the bearded man possibly wearing a monk's habit. The video then cuts to a full-screen view of the dark painting, emphasizing its oppressive atmosphere, before a brief, unrelated shot of a bright, pastoral scene, 'The Picnic' by Goya (1776), appears. Returning to the dark painting, the narrator recalls Goya's statement that his teachers were 'nature, Rembrandt and Velázquez,' emphasizing Goya's worship of Velázquez. This leads into a split-screen comparison of two full-length portraits: on the left, Francisco Goya’s 1788 portrait of Francisco Cabarrús in a green coat and white breeches, and on the right, Diego Velázquez’s 1635 portrait of Pablo de Valladolid in a dark, puffed-sleeve doublet, both standing in similar poses. The narrator confirms that Goya's portrait of Francisco Cabarrús 'is said to have been based on the Velázquez portrait of Pablo de Valladolid.' The video concludes with a return to the dark painting, now shown in a split-screen with a slightly different, mirrored version of itself, as the narrator suggests comparing it to a flipped version, reinforcing its haunting, symmetrical composition and the unsettling presence of the demonic figure.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a painted portrait of Salvador Martínez Cubells, an older man with a white beard, glasses, and a dark suit, identified as painted by Enrique Diosayudo in 1901. A male narrator explains that it is "thanks to Cubells that we the public have access to them today." The scene transitions through a sepia-toned archival photograph of a chaotic scene to a wide view of the ornate Spanish pavilion at the 1878 Exposition Universelle in Paris, where a large painting titled 'Witches’ Coven' is highlighted. The narrator reveals that Baron Émile d'Erlanger, who bought Goya's house, initially planned to sell the works at this exhibition. However, due to Cubells being a "painfully slow worker," only four of the fourteen paintings were transferred by 1875. This prompted d'Erlanger to change his mind and donate them to the Spanish government, as a black-and-white archival shot of the Prado Museum’s neoclassical facade appears, accompanied by the start of a low, melancholic string instrument playing in the background. The video then presents a side-by-side comparison of two dark, expressive paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series, 'Men Reading' and 'Women Laughing,' with their dimensions overlaid. The narrator suggests looking at these "companion pieces" together, noting they were originally next to each other in the farmhouse. He describes them as "smaller in scale than the others," "vertical," and having a "lighter theme, despite being chromatically darker," also sharing an "identical lighting source." The segment concludes with black-and-white X-ray or infrared images of the same two paintings, revealing underlying details, as the narrator mentions their "poor state" makes them "difficult to interpret," with the melancholic music continuing throughout.

A speaker discusses the conservation of such works, noting how original paint was lost during transfer from wall to canvas. The scene transitions to a close-up of Goya's 'Women Laughing Painting,' a dark, unsettling oil work depicting two women with wide, distorted grins, their features blurred and eerie, evoking madness or mockery. The camera slowly zooms in on the unsettling expressions and thick, expressive brushwork as the speaker identifies it as 'the key to this pair of paintings,' describing it as 'unusual for Goya' due to its 'overtly sexual' nature, showing 'a man pleasuring himself as two hags or prostitutes laugh at him,' suggesting Goya, being 'reserved and prudish,' 'only created [it] in secret.' A split-screen then juxtaposes the 'Women Laughing Painting' with two stark black-and-white etchings by Goya from 1799, 'It is Well Pulled Up' and 'Trials,' rendered with fine lines and dramatic shading, highlighting the contrast in medium. The focus shifts to 'Men Reading (From the Black Paintings),' a dark, expressive oil painting showing a group of six men 'shrouded in darkness,' huddled around a light source. The camera zooms in on the central figure, a man with a beard and white shirt, holding what the speaker identifies as a 'newspaper or magazine' on his lap, his shirt illuminated by a 'triangle of light' that directs the viewer's gaze. The speaker explains the men 'gather around the central character in order to hear him better,' noting that 'two more men are in the background,' with one gazing upwards having been 'added by the restorer.' A split-screen comparison follows, showing the current version of 'Men Reading' alongside a grainy 1874 photograph, revealing a more intact version with a clearer, circled face in the upper left that is now obscured. The video concludes with a full-frame view of the 'Men Reading' painting, emphasizing its dark, chaotic atmosphere and expressive, almost violent brushwork, with figures’ faces contorted in concentration and madness against a nearly black background, a work also known as 'the politicians.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Goya's dark, expressive painting 'Men Reading (From the Black Paintings)', depicting men with grim expressions, as the narrator suggests they are discussing a newspaper article about themselves, and that historian Francisco Javier de Salas compared the incessant talk of politicians in this work to the solitary pleasure of the man mocked by two women. The scene transitions to a split-screen, showing 'Men Reading' alongside the unsettling 'Women Laughing Painting', which depicts two women with wide, eerie grins, while somber, dramatic background music begins. The focus then shifts to a close-up of Goya’s 'Judith and Holofernes Painting', showing a woman in a golden headdress with a sword and a calm expression, as the narrator explains this depicts the Old Testament moment when Judith and her maidservant cut off the head of the tyrant Holofernes, with on-screen text quoting 'Judith 13:8 Then she struck his neck twice with all her might, and cut off his head'. This is followed by a comparison with Artemisia Gentileschi’s 1612 version of 'Judith and Holofernes', a more dynamic and violent depiction, which the narrator notes is often seen in art as a story of triumph over tyranny, a subject he discusses in his film about Gentileschi. The video then returns to Goya’s version, zooming in on Judith’s face and sword, emphasizing her unsettling calmness, as the narrator explains Goya moves away from traditional portraits, removing specific settings and peripheral iconographies to focus solely on the action, adding that the Judith Goya portrays could easily be a woman from his own time, highlighting this as a key point. A split-screen then juxtaposes Goya’s 'Judith and Holofernes' with the softer, serene 'The Milkmaid of Bordeaux', before lingering on Goya’s Judith, as the narrator concludes that the immediacy of the portrayal reminds us of Goya's history with Ferdinand VII.

The video concludes with a wide, static shot of the full *Fight with Cudgels* painting, reinforcing its chaotic energy and expressive brushwork, as the speaker interprets the scene of brutal and pointless violence as an allusion to the upheaval in Spain.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a grotesque, darkly painted face from Francisco Goya’s *Altropos (The Fates)*, emphasizing its distorted features, wide eye, and thick, expressive brushwork in earthy tones against a black background, as a male narrator highlights that "the brushwork is exactly how Goya intended" and that "the artist is at his most impressionistic here." The camera then pans down to reveal a hand holding a spoon, rendered with the same heavy, textured strokes, visually suggesting a connection to the *Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting*, while the narrator describes the paint being "handled quickly, but with the kind of firm hand you only get with a lifetime of experience." Melancholic background music begins to play. The scene transitions to a full view of *Altropos (The Fates)*, showing three dark, emaciated figures in a desolate landscape, each engaged in a grim task, as the narrator introduces the subject as Goya's "interpretation of a mythological subject, the Moirai, or the sisters of fate from classical literature." The painting is then compared side-by-side with a classical engraving of the same mythological trio—Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos—highlighting Goya’s grotesque reinterpretation of the traditional, idealized figures. The narrator details Clotho, "the youngest sister," who usually spins the thread of life but Goya depicts holding a newborn baby; Lachesis, "the second sister," holding a looking glass to inspect the thread; and Altropos, "the eldest sister," carrying scissors to cut the thread of life. The video zooms in on each figure, contrasting Goya’s dark, monstrous forms with the engraving’s smooth, classical anatomy, with a white outline then isolating the central figure, Lachesis, emphasizing her distorted, almost skeletal form. The narrator concludes by noting Goya's addition of a "fourth figure, not featured in the myth, a man with his hands tied behind his back," pondering if this represents Goya himself, "helpless to his fate, controlled by outside forces," as the overall mood of decay, fate, and psychological horror is underscored by the stark contrast between Goya’s dark vision and the Enlightenment-era idealism of the engraving.

segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment presents a curated sequence of Francisco Goya’s portraits, primarily focusing on women, progressing chronologically and stylistically from his later, more intimate works to earlier, formal pieces. It begins with *The Milkmaid of Bordeaux (Believed to be Leocadia)*, a soft, impressionistic 1827 painting showing a woman in profile with dark hair and a light headscarf, her face gently illuminated against a muted greenish background. This transitions to a sketchy, monochromatic drawing titled *I am Still Learning* (1826), depicting a woman in a white dress kneeling before an old man, rendered in loose charcoal strokes. The next image, *A Woman Kneeling Before an Old Man* (1820), continues the sketchy style, showing a similar scene with more defined figures. The sequence then shifts to *La Leocadia* (1827), a somber, dark painting of a woman in a black dress leaning over a stone ledge, her face illuminated against a pale sky, with a metal railing in the background. This is followed by *Majas on a Balcony* (1812), a richly detailed painting of two women in ornate golden dresses with lace veils, leaning on a black railing, their expressions serene and composed. The final images are *The White Duchess* (1795), a full-length portrait of a woman in a white dress with a red sash and bow, standing in a landscape with a small dog at her feet, and a split-screen comparison with *The Black Duchess* (1797), where the same woman is depicted in a black dress with gold trim, maintaining the same pose and background. The lighting evolves from soft and intimate in the later works to brighter and more formal in the earlier portraits, reflecting Goya’s stylistic range and emotional depth. On-screen text consistently identifies each painting with its title, artist, and year, anchoring the visual narrative in historical context.

A male speaker describes her as wearing a 'low cut, maha outfit,' an 'erotic proletariat type' that appealed to Goya, leaning against what looks to be a burial mound, possibly Goya's grave, mourning his end. She is interpreted as the personification of melancholy, meditating on life and death, and is considered one of Goya's final works in the series.
audio_updated_entities.X-Rays: High-contrast, monochrome images revealing hidden layers beneath the surface of the *La Leocadia Painting*, showing a faint, ghostly figure and compositional changes, with visible cracks and texture. A speaker explains these investigations revealed she originally leaned on a door or fireplace and had no veil, suggesting Goya later added a darker, more despondent theme.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the dynamic, surreal painting 'Fantastic Vision,' which the narrator identifies as depicting two floating figures—one male and one female—both appearing terrified. The female covers her face, looking away as if being chased, while the male points towards a distant rocky landscape with a settlement atop a cliff, which the narrator suggests is possibly the Rock of Gibraltar, a refuge for Spanish liberals during the Peninsular War. As the camera slowly pans right, revealing a chaotic crowd of tiny figures on horseback and on foot below, the narrator explains they are refugees running from danger, with the French army from the 3rd of May 1808 in the foreground, taking aim at the defenseless crowd. A somber, melancholic plucked string instrument begins to play as the scene transitions to a split-screen comparison of two dark, violent paintings, both showing figures with rifles rendered in thick, expressive brushwork. This is followed by a cut to 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' a dark, visceral painting of a gaunt, wild-eyed figure devouring a child, rendered in stark red and black contrasts. The narrator describes this as 'the most famous of the black paintings,' calling it 'the most horrific and the most disgust,' and noting it was probably more shocking to 19th-century Spanish audiences. The video then transitions to Henry Fuseli's 'The Nightmare' (1781), depicting a woman on a bed with a demonic figure perched on her chest, rendered in dramatic chiaroscuro. The final frames shift to a black-and-white, grainy archival clip of a man resembling a vampire leaning over a bed, with on-screen text stating 'Frankenstein' was published in 1818 and 'The Vampyre' in 1819. The narrator concludes by contextualizing these visuals within 'the rise of Romanticism and its obsession with the gory and the supernatural.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed black-and-white engraving of a chaotic, theatrical scene, possibly a witches’ sabbath, featuring a central demonic figure with horns, surrounded by writhing, grotesque figures under a dark, draped ceiling. A male narrator sets the context, mentioning the "Gothic novel" and "Phantasmagoria," a popular entertainment in Madrid where "images of monsters and ghoulish subject matter were projected in theaters using lanterns." The scene transitions to a dark, abstract, and rapidly moving image of a winged, monstrous figure in distorted, colorful brushstrokes, followed by a close-up of Francisco Goya's *Saturn Devouring His Son Painting*. The narrator describes the "cannibalism angle" as "particularly grotesque" and "even more horrifying that Goya painted it on the walls of his dining room." The visual then shifts to a dimly lit corridor with two framed paintings depicting violent mythological scenes. The narrator explains that "the story is from the mythological tale of the Greek god Cronos," whom "the Romans called him Saturn," who "overthrew his father" and, "afraid that history would repeat itself, he ate, or rather swallowed whole, five out of his six children." This mythological context is then illustrated by a series of classical paintings: Giovanni Romanelli's *Chronos and his Child* (1650s), Giorgio Vasari/Cristoforo Gherardi's *The Mutilation of Uranus by Saturn* (16th century), and an ancient Greek vase (460–450 BCE) showing Cronos handing a baby to a woman. The video returns to Goya's *Saturn Devouring His Son Painting*, focusing on the monstrous face, as the narrator notes that while "it has been tackled before by artists," "Goya's version is different." Peter Paul Rubens' *Saturn* (1636) is then shown, depicting a muscular, bearded Saturn holding a child about to devour it. The narrator concludes by emphasizing that in Goya's painting, "Saturn is not swallowing the body whole, but gnawing down on it in a frenzied and horrific attack," and that "it is not a baby's body, as in the myth, but a grown adult's, which makes it even more disturbing," as the final frames zoom in on the child’s body being consumed in Goya's work, emphasizing the visceral horror with red and white paint strokes.

audio_updated_entities.Cronos Handed a Baby Vase: An ancient Greek red-figure vase (460–450 BCE) showing a bearded Cronos handing a baby to a woman, rendered in stylized, orange figures on a black background. This depicts an aspect of the mythological tale of Cronos (Roman Saturn) who, fearing his children would overthrow him, ate them, though in some versions, a stone was substituted for a child.
audio_updated_entities.Saturn Painting (Rubens): A 1636 oil painting by Peter Paul Rubens depicting a muscular, bearded Saturn holding a child in his arms, about to devour it, with a star above, rendered in dramatic lighting and rich colors. This represents an earlier artistic interpretation of the mythological tale of Cronos (Roman Saturn) who ate his children, a version the narrator contrasts with Goya's later, more disturbing depiction.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Goya's 1797 red chalk drawing, 'Time Devouring Men,' depicting a monstrous, skeletal figure with long hair devouring a human form, rendered in expressive, sketchy lines, which the male narrator confirms was intended to depict an adult. The scene transitions to Goya's dark, visceral oil painting, 'Saturn Devouring His Son,' focusing on the gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair, its mouth agape as it consumes a child, rendered with thick, expressive brushwork and stark contrasts of red and black. The narrator describes Saturn's terrifying expression, emphasizing that he is not reveling in revenge but is shocked by his own monstrousness, fully aware of the terrible act he commits against his own flesh and blood, yet unable to stop himself. The camera slowly zooms in on the figure’s wide, staring eyes and the bloody, fleshy limb being devoured. The horrifying theme continues as the scene shifts to a chaotic, dark painting of a violent struggle with a dead man and distressed figures, followed by the black-and-white etching 'This is What You Were Born For' (published 1864), showing a man stabbing a dead body amidst a pile of corpses. Another etching, 'This is Worse' (published 1864), depicts a man in despair clutching his head. The narrator connects the 'raw flesh' in the paintings to the horrors Goya witnessed in Madrid: innocent citizens cut down, a mass of starving bodies, and piles of corpses left to rot, stating that Goya transcribed the 'hell on earth' he saw for future generations. The segment concludes with a full view of the 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting,' reinforcing its nightmarish horror, as the narrator suggests the painting is primarily about power and the extent to which someone will go, before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with 'The Execution of José María Torrijos Painting,' a grim historical scene of blindfolded men on a beach, with soldiers and bodies, accompanied by on-screen text detailing the 1831 execution ordered by Ferdinand VII. A male voice immediately begins discussing "pain power," drawing a parallel between "Saturn, like Ferdinand," who "used power not only to mistreat his own citizens, hence the adult corpse, but also to violently turn on those people." The speaker continues, asserting that "those in power will use any means possible to hang on to that power, including eating their own children." The scene then transitions to a close-up of a grotesque, wide-eyed face from another painting, emphasizing horror and suffering, as melancholy, eerie string music begins. The male voice shifts focus, stating that for him, "the most powerful image is not Saturn devouring his son, but the drowning dog. The most pessimistic and most wretched image of them all." The video cuts to 'The Drowning Dog Painting,' showing a small dog’s head barely above dark, murky water against a desolate background, with the title '23. The Drowning Dog.' The camera slowly zooms in on the dog’s desperate expression, while the speaker describes it as "a dog's head gazing upwards, lost in the vastness of the rest of the image, alone and drowning slowly in quicksand," looking "at someone or something outside of the composition." The image then shifts to a monochrome X-ray view, revealing faint, hidden marks above the dog’s head, circled for emphasis. A split-screen comparison follows, showing the original painting and the X-ray. The speaker explains that "in the original photo, it was discovered that the dog is looking at two birds flying in the sky, which disappeared from the painting when it was transferred from the walls to the canvas," before his speech is cut off. The overall atmosphere is somber and introspective, exploring themes of death, hidden meaning, and artistic technique, underscored by the continuous melancholic music.

A male narrator states that for this dog, there is "no salvation, no hope, and there is no God," explaining that "This dog is Goya's stand-in for humanity, struggling alone," looking to the sky for a miracle that "will never come." The narrator reveals that Goya left Spain at 78 to start over in France, dying in Bordeaux in 1828 as "a patriot in exile." This leads to Francisco Goya’s stark black-and-white etching *Truth Has Died*, depicting a lifeless nude woman surrounded by a distressed crowd, which the narrator implicitly links to Goya's "black paintings" where "there is no sense that the sacrifice of human life is going to lead to salvation." The video then shows a modern bronze statue of Goya in Bordeaux before a classical building, before cutting to a grainy, black-and-white archival photograph from the early 20th century, showing men gathered around a monument, with one in a white coat, as the narrator begins to describe "a macabre twist to the Goya story" in 1901.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Pieter Claesz’s somber *Vanitas Still Life*, featuring a human skull, books, a glass, a pipe, and an hourglass, all rendered in muted tones under chiaroscuro lighting, symbolizing mortality, as a melancholic string melody begins to play. This transitions to grainy black-and-white archival footage from the front page of the Spanish newspaper *ABC* (November 30, 1919), showing men in early 20th-century attire gathered around a stone monument, while a narrator explains that Goya's body was exhumed for transfer back to Spain, but his skull was mysteriously missing. The narrator continues, stating that "the painter," so skilled at understanding others, eventually lost his own head. The visuals then show a formal portrait of Charles III in Hunting Dress, followed by a close-up of Goya’s confident *Self-Portrait at an Easel*. The narrator identifies Goya as "the most radical artist that ever lived," "the last of the old masters and the first of the moderns," emphasizing "truth" as his defining characteristic. As the scene shifts to Goya's dark, expressive *Two Old Men Painting*, then the powerful *The Third of May 1808* depicting a man before a firing squad, and jarring modern news footage of a fiery explosion with a 'CIVILIANS UNDER ATTACK' chyron, the narrator describes Goya as a "prescient artist" and "chronicler of a violent age" who presented "uncomfortable truths" relevant even today. The segment concludes with a close-up of Goya’s visceral *Saturn Devouring His Son*, as the narrator reflects that Goya showed us "ordinary people are capable of evil" and can do "extraordinary things," suggesting that sometimes, when looking for monsters, we should look... (the audio fades out).

entity_library.Fight with Cudgels Painting: A dark, expressive oil painting by Francisco Goya showing two men in rough clothing engaged in a violent struggle with sticks, set against a rocky landscape, rendered in rough, dynamic brushwork with visible blood on one man’s neck, visually representing a 'picture of tribalism, a country tearing itself apart,' which the voiceover interprets as Goya's statement, 'this is Spain.'
entity_library.Classical Engraving of the Fates: A detailed, classical engraving depicting the three Fates—Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos—as idealized, nude or semi-nude figures in flowing drapery, seated on rocks, with labels beneath each figure, rendered in soft, naturalistic tones. It is used for direct comparison with Goya’s dark reinterpretation, representing the traditional depiction of the Fates whose roles (spinning, inspecting, cutting the thread of life) are described by the narrator.
entity_library.Little Giants Painting: A bright, pastoral oil painting by Francisco Goya (1792) showing three children playing, one riding on another’s shoulders, with the child on top wearing a red jacket and wide-brimmed hat, set against a pale sky and green landscape; the painting is shown first in full, then with the children removed, leaving only the adult figure.
entity_library.The Night of Enitharmon’s Joy Painting: A mystical, dark painting by William Blake (1795) depicting nude figures in a rocky, otherworldly landscape, with a central woman with long black hair and a serene expression, rendered in earthy tones with a dark, starry background.
entity_library.La Leocadia Painting: A somber, dark 1827 painting of a woman in a long black dress leaning over a stone ledge, her face illuminated against a pale, cloudy sky, with a metal railing visible in the background, evoking melancholy and introspection. A male speaker describes her as wearing a 'low cut, maha outfit,' an 'erotic proletariat type' that appealed to Goya, leaning against what looks to be a burial mound, possibly Goya's grave, mourning his end. She is interpreted as the personification of melancholy, meditating on life and death, and is considered one of Goya's final works in the series.
entity_library.(Identified as) Leocadia Painting: A formal portrait of a woman with curly hair in a lighter, more formal dress, painted by Francisco Goya in 1815, shown in split-screen comparison with 'La Leocadia.' The narrator states that most historians believe this well-known portrait is of Leocadia, and the 'black painting' (La Leocadia) bears a strong resemblance to it.
entity_library.Josefa Bayeu (Goya’s wife) Portrait: A black-and-white profile sketch from 1805, rendered in fine, textured lines, showing Goya’s wife with her hair styled in an updo, her face in profile with a serene expression. The narrator mentions her death as the event that led to Leocadia moving in with Goya.
entity_library.Goya’s last apartment in Bordeaux: A modern-day exterior shot of a stone building with a plaque reading 'COURS DE L’INTENDANCE' and a bronze bust of Goya, indicating the location of his final residence in Bordeaux. The narrator explains that Goya abandoned Spain for France in 1824, and Leocadia followed him with her daughter Rosario, staying there until his death in 1828.
entity_library.Goya in Bordeaux (Film Clip): A dimly lit interior scene from the 1999 film 'Goya in Bordeaux' by Carlos Saura, showing a group of people gathered around a piano in a room with a chandelier, evoking a somber, intimate atmosphere.
entity_library.Self-Portrait (Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla, 1: A black-and-white etching of a woman with a wide-brimmed hat and delicate features, rendered in fine, expressive lines, with a crown-like emblem in the upper left corner. The narrator identifies her as Leocadia's daughter, Rosario, whom Goya taught how to paint, and who later had a career as a painter in France and Spain. Many, including the narrator, believe she is Goya's illegitimate child.
entity_library.Portrait of Goya (Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla: A black-and-white profile etching of an aged Goya, showing his contemplative face with deep lines, receding hair, and a high collar, rendered in fine, expressive lines. This portrait was created by Rosaria Weiss Zorrilla, Leocadia's daughter, whom Goya taught to paint.
entity_library.I am Still Learning Painting: A sketchy, monochromatic 1826 drawing of a woman in a white dress kneeling before an old man, rendered in loose charcoal strokes with minimal detail and a dark, undefined background.
entity_library.A Woman Kneeling Before an Old Man Paint: A sketchy, monochromatic 1820 drawing showing a woman in a white dress kneeling before an old man, with more defined figures than the previous sketch, rendered in dark, expressive lines.
entity_library.Majas on a Balcony Painting: A richly detailed 1812 painting of two women in ornate golden dresses with lace veils, leaning on a black railing, their expressions serene and composed, set against a dark background. The narrator explains that Goya was fixated on the 'Maja's' elaborate outfits, sense of style in dress, and cheeky behavior, as depicted here.
entity_library.The White Duchess Painting: A full-length 1795 portrait of the Duchess of Alba, one of Spain's leading nobles, in a white dress with a red sash and bow, standing in a landscape with a small dog at her feet, her dark hair adorned with a red flower, rendered in bright, formal lighting. The narrator identifies her as the Duchess of Alba, dressed in elegant and expensive French fashions of the day, and reveals Goya's infatuation with her.
entity_library.The Black Duchess Painting: A full-length 1797 portrait of the Duchess of Alba, the same woman as in *The White Duchess*, now in a black dress with gold trim, maintaining the same pose and background, creating a stark contrast in color and mood. The narrator notes that Goya painted her in this portrait two years later, hinting at a 'scandalous' depiction.
entity_library.21. Fantastic Vision: A dynamic, surreal oil painting depicting two figures in mid-motion, one draped in red, pointing toward a distant rocky landscape with a settlement atop a cliff, while a crowd of tiny figures gathers below, rendered in earthy tones and expressive brushwork. A speaker identifies this painting, also known by its alternative title, as having been named by Goya's friend, Antonio Brugad.
entity_library.Asmodeus (Dictionnaire Infernal, 1818): A black-and-white engraving showing a demonic figure with a pig-like snout and horns, flanked by other grotesque beings, rendered in dense cross-hatching, with the text 'Asmodeus Dictionnaire Infernal, 1818' in the lower left corner. A speaker explains that in Jewish legend, Asmodeus was the prince of demons and hell.
entity_library.Fantastic Vision: A dynamic, surreal oil painting depicting two floating figures, one male and one female, both seemingly terrified. The female covers her face and looks away as if being chased, while the male points to a town at the top of a mountain, identified by the narrator as possibly the Rock of Gibraltar, a refuge for Spanish liberals during the Peninsular War. The crowd below, with horses and wagons, are described by the narrator as refugees fleeing danger, with the French army from the 3rd of May 1808 in the foreground, taking aim at them.
entity_library.The Nightmare: A dark, dramatic painting by Henry Fuseli (1781) depicting a woman lying on a bed with a demonic figure perched on her chest, rendered in dramatic chiaroscuro with a dark, shadowy atmosphere, which the narrator links to the rise of Romanticism and its obsession with the gory and the supernatural.
entity_library.On-Screen Text: White text overlay stating 'Frankenstein' was published in 1818 and 'The Vampyre' in 1819, which the narrator uses to link the visual horror to literary Gothic traditions and the rise of Romanticism and its obsession with the gory and the supernatural.
entity_library.Chronos and his Child Painting: A Baroque painting by Giovanni Romanelli (1650s) depicting a winged, bearded Chronos holding a scythe and a child, rendered in dramatic chiaroscuro with a cloudy sky background. This illustrates the mythological tale of the Greek god Cronos, whom the Romans called Saturn, who overthrew his father and, fearing a similar fate, ate five out of his six children.
entity_library.The Mutilation of Uranus by Saturn Paint: A 16th-century fresco by Giorgio Vasari/Cristoforo Gherardi depicting a violent mythological scene with a large celestial globe, a figure holding a scythe, and other figures in torment. This painting illustrates the mythological tale of the Greek god Cronos (Roman Saturn) who overthrew his father.
entity_library.Cronos Handed a Baby Vase: An ancient Greek red-figure vase (460–450 BCE) showing a bearded Cronos handing a baby to a woman, rendered in stylized, orange figures on a black background. This depicts an aspect of the mythological tale of Cronos (Roman Saturn) who, fearing his children would overthrow him, ate them, though in some versions, a stone was substituted for a child.
entity_library.Saturn Painting (Rubens): A 1636 oil painting by Peter Paul Rubens depicting a muscular, bearded Saturn holding a child in his arms, about to devour it, with a star above, rendered in dramatic lighting and rich colors. This represents an earlier artistic interpretation of the mythological tale of Cronos (Roman Saturn) who ate his children, a version the narrator contrasts with Goya's later, more disturbing depiction.
entity_library.Time Devouring Men Painting: A red chalk drawing by Francisco Goya (1797) showing a monstrous, skeletal figure with long hair devouring a human form, rendered in expressive, sketchy lines with a focus on the act of consumption, which the narrator confirms was intended to depict an adult.
entity_library.This is What You Were Born For Etching: A black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a man standing over a pile of dead bodies, one of which he is stabbing with a knife, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic contrast. This image, along with others, is thematically linked by the narrator to the 'hell on earth' Goya witnessed in Madrid, including innocent citizens cut down and piles of corpses.
entity_library.The Execution of José María Torrijos Pai: A historical oil painting depicting a grim execution scene on a beach with blindfolded men, soldiers, and bodies, accompanied by on-screen text about the 1831 execution ordered by Ferdinand VII. The accompanying male voice draws a parallel between Ferdinand and Saturn, discussing the abuse of "pain power" and mentioning the "adult corpse" in the context of those in power mistreating citizens.
entity_library.The Drowning Dog Painting: A dark, minimalist oil painting titled '23. The Drowning Dog,' showing only the head of a small dog barely above murky water against a desolate background. The male voice describes it as "the most pessimistic and most wretched image of them all," depicting the dog "gazing upwards, lost in the vastness... alone and drowning slowly in quicksand," looking "at someone or something outside of the composition." The audio further reveals that X-rays show the dog was originally looking at two birds flying in the sky, which disappeared when the painting was transferred from walls to canvas.
entity_library.The Last Judgment: A dark, surreal oil painting by Hieronymus Bosch (1486) showing a chaotic scene with a fish-headed creature, a bowl with nude figures, a monkey fishing from a cage, and other grotesque elements, rendered in earthy tones and intricate detail, setting a tone of horrific imagery as described by the narrator.
entity_library.Truth Has Died: A stark black-and-white etching by Francisco Goya (published 1864) depicting a nude, lifeless woman lying on the ground, surrounded by a crowd of distressed figures, rendered in dense cross-hatching and expressive lines. The narrator implicitly links this work to Goya's 'black paintings,' where there is 'no sense that the sacrifice of human life is going to lead to salvation.'
entity_library.Goya statue in Bordeaux: A modern bronze statue of Francisco Goya standing before a classical building with barred windows, wearing a long coat and holding a hat, positioned on a pedestal with a black iron fence in front, representing the artist in the city where he died in exile.
entity_library.Bordeaux: A sepia-toned aerial view of the city of Bordeaux, showing rooftops, buildings, and a prominent church spire, with the text 'Bordeaux' overlaid. The narrator explains that Francisco Goya, 'a patriot in exile,' died here in 1828 after leaving Spain at the age of 78.
entity_library.Vanitas Still Life: A somber oil painting by Pieter Claesz (1630) featuring a human skull on books, a glass, a pipe, and an hourglass, rendered in muted browns and grays under chiaroscuro lighting, symbolizing mortality.
entity_library.News Broadcast Footage: Modern, shaky video footage with a red and blue chyron reading 'DEVELOPING NOW: CIVILIANS UNDER ATTACK', showing a fiery explosion and people fleeing through rubble, creating a jarring contemporary parallel to Goya's historical depictions of violence and 'uncomfortable truths' about human nature, as highlighted by the narrator.
